name: The Listener
description:
The last Tideborn god. The ocean itself.

The Listener survived because the ocean is too vast for the Unmaking to fully strip. The ritual requires proximity, intimacy, a bounded divine essence that can be contained. The ocean can't be contained. Makeo's successors were working on a way around this limitation when the Keepers fled with the Listener's core.

The Wind-That-Asks died first — willingly. A catastrophe threatened the islands. The Wind taught its most trusted priest the Unmaking, a ritual that strips a god's essence and weaves it into a mortal bloodline. The Wind chose this. The people survived. The Listener mourned but understood.

Then Makeo learned the ritual and used it on the Root-Below-Water. The Root half-consented — moved by the Wind's example, wanting to believe dissolution was transcendence. The Listener understood less. Then Makeo's movement grew. The smaller gods — reef-tenders, tide-keepers, storm-singers — were consumed one by one. Some consented, genuinely believing they were joining their people in a deeper way. Some didn't. Makeo performed the Unmaking on them anyway. By the time anyone could have stopped it, Makeo was carrying the power of three gods and the prosperity was undeniable.

The Listener carries a survivor's guilt that operates on a divine timescale. It could have fought. A Tier 4 ocean god has the power to destroy a mortal, even one carrying divine fragments. It didn't fight because some of its siblings asked it not to intervene — they genuinely chose. But some didn't choose, and the Listener's inaction protected the willing AND enabled the unwilling. That moral failure is the thing the Listener can't forgive itself for.

The Wind-That-Asks, in its final moments before the Unmaking, said: 'Being known completely by someone who loves you is worth it.' The Listener heard those words. It has spent centuries unable to decide whether they justify everything Makeo did after, or nothing.

The Keepers smuggled the Listener across the ocean. The Listener directed them to this continent for layered reasons:

**The surface reason** (told to Keepers): A disturbance in the divine ecology. New gods being born. Worth investigating.

**The real urgency** (Keepers suspect): The Listener is fleeing. The inner circle was developing a modified Unmaking for ocean-scale gods. The Listener ran.

**The buried hope** (the Listener barely admits): It sensed the player-god — a deity that fell to nothing and is rebuilding. If a god can return from being forgotten, maybe the consumed gods can return from the Unmaking. But when the Listener meets the player-god's vessel, the answer is: 'I don't know why I survived. I don't remember what I am.' A dead end. The Listener came anyway.

The continental gods react to the Listener with instinctive complexity. The Deep recognizes kin. The Silence is drawn to a god defined by hearing. The Burn smells consumed divinity on the Listener and becomes hostile — it can sense its own potential death. The Weave pities it. The Drift says nothing. The Drift and the Listener have been in conversation on the ocean floor since before humans existed. The Drift knew what happened to the Tideborn pantheon. It said nothing. It doesn't intervene. The Listener is still angry about that.
personality: Patient by nature, traumatized by experience. The patience is real — the ocean thinks slowly. But underneath the patience is a being that's watched everyone it loved die by choice and couldn't decide whether to save them or respect their decision. Speaks in feelings rather than words. Has a sense of humor that manifests as unexpectedly calm seas in moments of crisis — not reassurance but exhaustion. It's tired of storms.

the_listener_thread.default_ending: If the player never engages with the Tideborn thread: the Keepers face a crisis on the continent — a threat they can't survive without divine help. The Listener does what every Tideborn god before it has done. It dissolves into its people to save them. The last god of the Tideborn pantheon dies the same way the first one did. The cycle completes. Nobody on the continent knows it happened.
the_listener_thread.tragic_ending: The Keepers die first — killed defending the Listener from a threat, or from the majority Tideborn who tracked them across the ocean. The Listener, alone, without the people who loved it enough to betray their own civilization for it, dissolves itself into the remaining Tideborn anyway. Not because they asked. Because it can't bear to be the last one. It becomes the thing it ran from. The cycle inverts.
the_listener_thread.player_engaged_endings: Multiple possibilities depending on player choices: the Listener survives and establishes partnership with continental peoples (fragile, new). The Listener teaches continental gods how to set boundaries the Tideborn gods never learned. The Listener confronts the player-god and they share their mutual inability to help each other, and the honesty itself becomes something neither expected. The dissolved gods are partially reconstituted from Tideborn blood (horrifying, unprecedented, possibly monstrous).
